question.text: Under which of the following circumstances can an open offer, once made, be withdrawn?

answer.text: When the target company's stock price drops by more than 25%
When the acquirer finds adverse information during due diligence
When a statutory approval required for the acquisition is refused
When the acquirer receives a better acquisition opportunity elsewhere

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Under Regulation 23, an open offer can be withdrawn only under limited circumstances: statutory approval required but refused (e.g., CCI or FIPB), conditions in the agreement making performance impossible/illegal, death of the acquirer (if individual), or SAT/court order. **The Trap:** Stock price drops and adverse due diligence findings are NOT valid grounds for withdrawal — the regulation is deliberately restrictive to protect shareholders who have already acted in reliance on the offer. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series IX (Merchant Banking) and Series XXI (Corporate Governance) both test the binding nature of open offers and permissible withdrawal conditions. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Once an open offer is triggered, treat it as virtually irrevocable — the withdrawal grounds are so narrow that acquirers should complete all due diligence before triggering the offer.
